defenseModes.label: Inductive
defenseModes.note: The rule is defended from observed regularity, so parallel regularities deserve comparable treatment.

defenseModes.label: Abductive
defenseModes.note: Best-explanation claims still owe rival-hypothesis comparison and specificity accounting.

defenseModes.label: Modal
defenseModes.note: Observed invariance has been promoted toward necessity; the bridge premise needs independent support.

defenseModes.label: Metaphysical
defenseModes.note: The argument has shifted from observation to substantive metaphysics; ordinary induction no longer carries the full burden.

defenseModes.label: Analytic
defenseModes.note: If the rule is definitional, it may not license a substantive conclusion without an added bridge premise.

defenseModes.label: Rhetorical
defenseModes.note: Rhetorical obviousness is not a substitute for scope, projectibility, and defeater checks.

differentiatorTypes.label: None supplied
differentiatorTypes.warning: The parallel is being discounted without a visible differentiator.

differentiatorTypes.label: Independent support
differentiatorTypes.warning: This can justify asymmetry if the support really changes an evidential variable.

differentiatorTypes.label: Scope distinction
differentiatorTypes.warning: A scope distinction helps only if the target domain is not being used selectively.

differentiatorTypes.label: Evidence quality
differentiatorTypes.warning: Quality differences matter when reliability, specificity, or replicability genuinely differ.

differentiatorTypes.label: Defeater supplied
differentiatorTypes.warning: A defeater helps when it undercuts the parallel without also undercutting the preferred rule.

differentiatorTypes.label: Mere assertion
differentiatorTypes.warning: A stated exception is not yet an evidentially relevant difference.

differentiatorTypes.label: Circular
differentiatorTypes.warning: The differentiator appears to restate the preferred conclusion.

differentiatorTypes.label: Modal smuggling
differentiatorTypes.warning: An observed regularity is being treated as necessity without a bridge premise.

differentiatorTypes.label: Specificity inflation
differentiatorTypes.warning: The claim has gained doctrinal content beyond what the original evidence supports.

patterns.title: Cosmological cause
patterns.claim: The universe began to exist; therefore, it had a cause, and that cause is God.
patterns.evidence: In ordinary experience, things that begin to exist or events that occur have causal antecedents.
patterns.rule: Whatever begins to exist has a cause.
repair: The universe raises questions about ultimate explanation.
Observed beginnings within the universe are commonly explained by prior physical conditions.
A theistic cause requires additional bridge premises beyond the causal regularity itself.

parallels.title: Known causes occur within spacetime
parallels.observation: All clear causal relations we observe occur within temporal and physical domains.
parallels.implication: A spacetime-external cause does not inherit support from ordinary causal cases without a bridge.

parallels.title: Known minds depend on physical brains
parallels.observation: Mental states reliably vary with brain states, injury, development, chemistry, and sleep.
parallels.implication: A disembodied divine mind runs against the same kind of observed regularity.

parallels.title: Known creators are embodied agents
parallels.observation: Every observed creator uses physical capacities, materials, tools, and temporal processes.
parallels.implication: A nonphysical creator outside the universe is not parallel to observed creators.

parallels.title: No observed creation from nothing
parallels.observation: Observed production transforms prior materials or energy rather than generating being from absolute nothing.
parallels.implication: Creation ex nihilo is not supported by ordinary creation analogies.

parallels.title: Observed entities are causally embedded
parallels.observation: Observed entities arise within networks of prior conditions, constraints, and dependencies.
parallels.implication: Stopping the causal demand at a preferred deity requires a principled exception.

patterns.title: Design from complexity
patterns.claim: Complex order in life or the cosmos is best explained by an intelligent designer.
patterns.evidence: Human artifacts with complex specified arrangement often result from intelligent activity.
patterns.rule: Complex specified arrangements are best explained by design.
repair: Design is one candidate only where the reference class resembles known design cases.
A supernatural designer requires added evidence for agency, embodiment, ability, and identity.

parallels.title: Known designers are products of natural processes
parallels.observation: Every observed designer has a biological, developmental, and cultural history.
parallels.implication: An unevolved designer is unlike the designers used in the analogy.

parallels.title: Natural processes produce order
parallels.observation: Self-organization, selection, gravity, chemistry, and feedback processes generate structured outcomes.
parallels.implication: Order alone does not select design over natural mechanisms.

parallels.title: Observed design uses physical media
parallels.observation: Designers communicate plans and manipulate materials through physical channels.
parallels.implication: A nonphysical designer acting without media needs a supported mechanism or bridge.

parallels.title: Design evidence underspecifies identity
parallels.observation: Evidence for generic design does not identify a specific religion, scripture, incarnation, or doctrine.
parallels.implication: Moving from designer to Christian theism adds a specificity burden.

patterns.title: Argument from consciousness
patterns.claim: Human consciousness is best explained by a divine mind or nonphysical mental reality.
patterns.evidence: Consciousness seems difficult to reduce fully to impersonal matter.
patterns.rule: Mind is better explained by mind than by nonmind.
repair: The difficulty does not by itself establish a disembodied divine mind.
Any mental explanation must still answer why observed minds track physical brains so tightly.

parallels.title: Mental life tracks brain activity
parallels.observation: Perception, memory, agency, mood, and personality shift with brain conditions.
parallels.implication: The strongest observed pattern connects minds to physical systems.

parallels.title: Consciousness develops over time
parallels.observation: Human consciousness emerges through biological development rather than appearing fully formed.
parallels.implication: Eternal uncreated consciousness lacks support from observed mental emergence.

parallels.title: Desire and will are biologically entangled
parallels.observation: Intentions and motivations are connected to needs, emotions, and neurochemical states.
parallels.implication: Attributing will to an immaterial being imports agency beyond the observed pattern.

parallels.title: Minds appear temporally structured
parallels.observation: Thinking, intending, responding, and knowing appear to involve sequence or change.
parallels.implication: A timeless mind needs an account of cognition without temporal process.

patterns.title: Revelation and testimony
patterns.claim: Religious testimony and sacred texts provide strong evidence for Christian revelation.
patterns.evidence: Testimony can preserve real events, teachings, and experiences across communities.
patterns.rule: Sincere testimony from a tradition can justify belief in the reported revelation.
repair: Testimony can be evidence when source quality is strong.
Religious testimony must be weighed against rival testimony, transmission limits, and defeaters.
A specific revelation requires more than sincerity or community preservation.

parallels.title: Rival traditions also have testimony
parallels.observation: Competing religions preserve sincere testimony for incompatible revelations and miracles.
parallels.implication: Sincerity and tradition alone cannot uniquely privilege Christian claims.

parallels.title: Divine experiences are psychologically variable
parallels.observation: Religious experiences vary by culture, expectation, practice, and prior belief.
parallels.implication: Experience reports require psychological and cultural rival explanations.

parallels.title: Communication uses physical media
parallels.observation: Communication occurs through bodies, sound, writing, gesture, images, or other physical channels.
parallels.implication: Revelation from an immaterial deity needs a credible transmission account.

parallels.title: Sacred texts carry human marks
parallels.observation: Texts show redaction, genre, cultural framing, tension, and historical limitations.
parallels.implication: Human authorship remains a live rival hypothesis.

patterns.title: Miracle reports
patterns.claim: Miracle reports provide evidence that God intervenes in the world.
patterns.evidence: Some reported events appear unusual, striking, or difficult to explain naturalistically.
patterns.rule: Anomalous reports can indicate supernatural intervention.
repair: An anomaly can justify further investigation.
A miracle conclusion requires strong source quality, rival exclusion, and cross-tradition consistency.
The theological identity of the cause requires evidence beyond the anomaly.

parallels.title: Miracle claims conflict across traditions
parallels.observation: Many incompatible religions report miracles supporting incompatible doctrines.
parallels.implication: Miracle testimony does not automatically identify a single theological system.

parallels.title: Physical regularities remain stable
parallels.observation: The world is overwhelmingly modeled by stable physical regularities rather than visible supernatural interruption.
parallels.implication: Intervention claims carry a heavy evidence-quality burden.

parallels.title: Miracle reports are often anecdotal
parallels.observation: Many reports lack controlled observation, independent replication, and robust documentation.
parallels.implication: Low evidence quality should reduce confidence even when the report is sincere.

parallels.title: Psychology explains many experiences
parallels.observation: Expectation, memory, stress, group pressure, and attribution biases shape extraordinary reports.
parallels.implication: Psychological rivals must be compared before supernatural inference is licensed.

patterns.title: Moral argument
patterns.claim: Objective morality requires God as its foundation.
patterns.evidence: Humans experience moral obligations as serious, binding, and not merely preferential.
patterns.rule: Binding moral obligation is best explained by a divine moral source.
repair: Moral experience is real evidence about human cognition and social life.
A divine source is one hypothesis, not an automatic entailment.
The argument must account for moral diversity, disagreement, and the content of alleged divine commands.

parallels.title: Moral codes vary and evolve
parallels.observation: Moral norms differ across cultures and change with social conditions, knowledge, and power.
parallels.implication: Moral experience may reflect human social construction and revision.

parallels.title: Sacred commands can conflict with moral judgment
parallels.observation: Some scriptural commands appear morally troubling under ordinary human moral assessment.
parallels.implication: A divine-source theory can inherit defeaters from the content it must defend.

parallels.title: Moral cognition tracks social minds
parallels.observation: Empathy, reciprocity, punishment, cooperation, and status all shape moral judgment.
parallels.implication: Human moral cognition has strong natural and social explanations.

parallels.title: Moral grounding underspecifies Christianity
parallels.observation: Even if morality had a transcendent ground, that would not identify a triune Christian God.
parallels.implication: Moving from moral realism to Christian theism adds a specificity burden.
